critic_raw: The multimodal post-operative complication prediction experiment has several issues. The composite outcome lumps six distinct complications. Explicit case balancing artificially alters class distribution. The 85% specificity threshold lacks clinical workflow grounding. Surgeon fixed effects may not generalize to new surgeons in the test set.
all_issues_raised: Composite outcome lumps six mechanistically distinct complications; per-complication performance not assessed
Explicit case balancing changes class distribution artificially; should be done via loss function not data selection
85% specificity threshold lacks clinical justification tied to workflow capacity
Surgeon fixed effect may not generalize to new surgeons appearing in the test set after the training window

round_transcripts.critic: The multimodal post-operative complication prediction experiment has several issues. The composite outcome lumps six distinct complications. Explicit case balancing artificially alters class distribution. The 85% specificity threshold lacks clinical workflow grounding. Surgeon fixed effects may not generalize to new surgeons in the test set.
round_transcripts.defender:
On composite outcome: composite endpoints are common in surgical complication prediction because individual complication types are rare; combining them ensures adequate positive class size. However, the critic is correct that a composite model may mask per-complication performance differences. The design should include stratified analysis by complication type as a secondary metric, which is partially addressed by the 'subgroup analysis by complication severity' mentioned in Section 7.

On explicit balancing: the phrase 'balance these against uncomplicated cases' likely refers to case-control sampling to ensure sufficient positive examples for model training, not synthetic oversampling. If it means stratified sampling that preserves realistic prevalence, this is acceptable. If it means deliberate 1:1 case-control matching, this changes the effective class distribution and should be noted in evaluation (reporting metrics at the appropriate prevalence, not the balanced one).

On 85% specificity threshold: the choice of 85% is not clinically grounded in the design. A clinically justified threshold would come from workflow capacity analysis. This is a real gap.

On surgeon fixed effects: the concern is valid. With a temporal split, new surgeons entering after the training window would have unknown fixed effects. The design should use shrinkage regularization or treat surgeon as a random effect rather than fixed, or exclude surgeon from the feature set and instead include surgeon-level aggregate statistics computed from training data only.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense concedes the 85% specificity threshold lacks clinical grounding and that the surgeon fixed effect generalization concern is real. Two issues remain unresolved: (1) the specificity threshold is unjustified clinically; and (2) surgeon fixed effects in a temporal split will encounter unseen surgeons in the test set with no basis for prediction.
round_transcripts.defender: Both points are valid unresolved gaps. The specificity threshold should be grounded in intervention capacity per clinical unit. The surgeon generalization problem requires either using random effects, aggregate statistics, or explicitly handling new-surgeon cases in the test set evaluation. These are not cosmetic issues — they affect the clinical applicability of the model's performance claims.

all_issues_adjudicated: 85% specificity threshold lacks clinical workflow justification
Surgeon fixed effects will not generalize to new surgeons appearing in temporal test set
